title: 15 TOXIC Foods That Can KILL Your Cat 🐱 NEVER Feed Them These
description: Complete list of the 15 most dangerous foods for cats according to veterinarians and the ASPCA. Chocolate, onion, garlic, grapes and more common foods that can be lethal to your cat. Learn the symptoms of poisoning and what to do in case of emergency. Vital information every cat owner should know.
tags: what not to feed your cat

segments.voice: Every year, the ASPCA emergency hotline receives more than three hundred thousand calls about pet poisoning, and cats account for nearly forty percent of them. The most shocking part: most poisonings are caused by common foods that owners share, not knowing they are lethal. Today, we may save your cat's life.
segments.visual: cat sniffing food on kitchen counter

segments.voice: Welcome to CatBrothers. This is probably the most important video you'll ever watch about feline care. We're going to walk through the fifteen most dangerous foods for cats according to the ASPCA, the Animal Poison Control Center, and multiple veterinary studies. Share this video with anyone who has a cat. It can save lives.
segments.visual: veterinarian examining cat health check

segments.voice: Number fifteen: avocado. It contains persin, a fungicidal toxin that is harmless to humans but can cause vomiting and diarrhea in cats. According to the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center, although avocado toxicity is mild compared to other foods on this list, repeated exposure can cause heart problems in felines.
segments.visual: avocado sliced on table near cat

segments.voice: Number fourteen: milk and dairy products. Surprised? Contrary to popular myth, most adult cats are lactose intolerant. Doctor Lori Teller from Texas A&M University explains that after weaning, cats lose the lactase enzyme needed to digest lactose. Giving them milk causes diarrhea, vomiting, and stomach upset.
segments.visual: cat drinking milk from bowl myth

segments.voice: Number thirteen: raw bread dough with yeast. When a cat ingests raw dough, the yeast ferments in its stomach producing ethanol and carbon dioxide. The stomach expands dangerously and the alcohol produced can cause ethyl intoxication. Veterinarian Tim Hackett at Colorado State University documented severe cases of gastric distension.
segments.visual: raw bread dough on kitchen counter

segments.voice: Number twelve: cooked bones. Raw bones can be acceptable under supervision, but cooked ones are extremely dangerous. When cooked, bones become brittle and can splinter into sharp fragments. The ASPCA warns these splinters can perforate the esophagus, stomach or intestines, causing potentially fatal internal bleeding.
segments.visual: cooked chicken bones on plate danger

segments.voice: Number eleven: alcohol. Cats are extremely sensitive to ethanol. According to Doctor Eric Dunayer, veterinary toxicologist, just one tablespoon of alcohol can cause severe liver damage in a medium-sized cat. Symptoms include vomiting, disorientation, breathing difficulty, and in serious cases, coma. Never leave alcoholic drinks within reach of your cat.
segments.visual: glass of wine on table cat nearby

segments.voice: Number ten: citrus fruits. Lemons, oranges, limes, and grapefruits contain essential oils and a compound called psoralen that is toxic to cats. The Animal Poison Control Center reports that citrus ingestion can cause vomiting, diarrhea, and central nervous system depression. Even the peel and the leaves of citrus trees are dangerous.
segments.visual: orange lemon citrus fruits on counter

segments.voice: Number nine: coffee and caffeine. Caffeine contains methylxanthines, compounds that are extremely dangerous for cats. Doctor Justine Lee, director of veterinary emergency medicine, explains that caffeine can cause tachycardia, muscle tremors, seizures, and death in cats. Coffee grounds and used tea bags are especially dangerous.
segments.visual: coffee cup and cat on kitchen table

segments.voice: Number eight: xylitol. This artificial sweetener found in gum, sugar-free candy, and some toothpastes is deadly to cats. A study published in the Journal of Veterinary Emergency and Critical Care showed that xylitol causes a massive insulin release in felines, triggering severe hypoglycemia that can be fatal within hours.
segments.visual: sugar free gum candy packets

segments.voice: Number seven: grapes and raisins. Although the exact mechanism of toxicity remains a mystery, the ASPCA has documented hundreds of cases of acute kidney failure in cats after ingesting grapes or raisins. A recent study from Massey University in New Zealand suggests tartaric acid may be responsible. Even a single grape can be dangerous.
segments.visual: grapes and raisins on table close up

segments.voice: Number six: raw eggs. Beyond the risk of Salmonella and E. coli, raw eggs contain avidin, a protein that interferes with the absorption of biotin, an essential B vitamin. Veterinary nutritionist Sean Delaney at the University of California Davis explains that biotin deficiency in cats causes skin, coat, and nervous system problems.
segments.visual: raw egg cracked in bowl kitchen

segments.voice: Number five: too much tuna. A little tuna occasionally is not toxic, but regular consumption can cause serious problems. Tuna lacks sufficient taurine, an amino acid essential for cats. Also, according to an FDA study, tuna accumulates mercury. Doctor Jennifer Coates warns that tuna addiction is real in cats and can lead to malnutrition.
segments.visual: can of tuna fish open on counter

segments.voice: Number four: garlic. Garlic is five times more toxic than onion to cats. It contains organosulfur compounds that destroy red blood cells, causing hemolytic anemia. Veterinary toxicologist Sharon Gwaltney-Brant documented that even small amounts of garlic powder can be dangerous. Symptoms can take days to appear, making diagnosis difficult.
segments.visual: garlic cloves on cutting board kitchen

segments.voice: Number three: onions and leeks. The entire Allium family is toxic to cats. They contain thiosulfate, which causes oxidation of hemoglobin and the formation of Heinz bodies in red blood cells. A study in the Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery documented that ingestion of just five grams per kilogram of body weight can cause potentially fatal anemia.
segments.visual: onions and leeks on kitchen counter

segments.voice: What's most dangerous about onions is that they're everywhere: soups, sauces, pizzas, ready-made meals. Veterinary toxicologist Ahna Brutlag warns that many owners poison their cats unknowingly by sharing leftover human food containing onion as an ingredient. Always check ingredients before sharing any food with your cat.
segments.visual: person sharing food with cat at table

segments.voice: Number two: chocolate. Chocolate contains theobromine and caffeine, both methylxanthines highly toxic to cats. Dark chocolate is the most dangerous: it contains up to ten times more theobromine than milk chocolate. The ASPCA reports the lethal dose of theobromine in cats is two hundred milligrams per kilogram, present in just fifty grams of dark chocolate.
segments.visual: dark chocolate bar pieces on table

segments.voice: Symptoms of chocolate poisoning include vomiting, diarrhea, fast breathing, seizures, and cardiac arrhythmias. They can appear between six and twelve hours after ingestion. Doctor Tim Hackett warns there is no specific antidote for theobromine poisoning, only supportive treatment. Prevention is the only solution.
segments.visual: veterinarian treating sick cat emergency

segments.voice: And number one, the most dangerous: lilies. Every part of the lily plant, including pollen and the vase water, is extremely toxic to cats. A Colorado State University study documented that ingesting even a small amount of lily petal can cause irreversible acute kidney failure in less than seventy-two hours.
segments.visual: lily flowers in vase on table

segments.voice: Doctor Cathy Langston at Ohio State University reported that lilies are the number one cause of acute kidney failure from poisoning in cats. The most terrifying part is that many owners don't know they are toxic and keep them as decoration at home. If you have a cat, remove every lily from your home immediately.
segments.visual: cat near houseplant danger warning

segments.voice: What should you do if you suspect your cat ate something toxic? First, stay calm. Second, identify what they ate and how much. Third, call your vet or the animal poison emergency hotline immediately. Never induce vomiting without professional guidance, since some toxins cause more damage when passing back through the esophagus.
segments.visual: person calling veterinarian phone emergency

segments.voice: Prevention is always the best medicine. Keep dangerous foods in closed cabinets, don't share human food without checking the ingredients, and educate everyone in your household about these dangers. A curious cat can find anything, so cat-proofing your kitchen is as important as childproofing your home.
segments.visual: cat proof kitchen secure cabinets

segments.voice: Always keep your emergency vet's number and the Animal Toxicology Center's number on hand. In the United States, the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center is available twenty-four seven. Doctor Steven Hansen, director of the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center, insists that response speed is the most decisive factor in the survival of a poisoned animal.
segments.visual: emergency vet contact information card

segments.voice: Remember these three foods above all: lilies, chocolate, and onions. They are the three most common killers according to ASPCA statistics. Share this video with any friend or family member who has a cat. This information can be the difference between life and death for a feline. We are not exaggerating.
segments.visual: healthy happy cat playing safe home

segments.voice: If you love cats as much as we do, subscribe to CatBrothers and hit the bell. What's coming next week is going to blow your mind. We're exploring the five most fascinating scientific theories on why cats knead. It goes deeper than you imagine. See you in the next documentary.
segments.visual: cute cat looking at camera friendly
